sections.heading: RT
sections.content:
RT is preferred in the management of vaginal cancer rather than surgery as primary treatment due to improved organ preservation. Preferred modalities for definitive management include either concurrent pelvic chemoradiation (platinum-based) + brachytherapy or EBRT + brachytherapy. The addition of brachytherapy to EBRT is preferred as the combination has been shown to improve disease control. Overall treatment time should not extend beyond 8 weeks and treatment delays/interruptions need to be minimized.

Principles:
- For the majority of vaginal cancers, radiation is used rather than surgery as primary treatment due to improved organ preservation
- The addition of brachytherapy to EBRT is preferred as the combination has been shown to improve disease control
- Overall treatment time should not extend beyond 8 weeks
- Treatment delays/interruptions need to be minimized
- Brachytherapy alone is not recommended for most tumors, even early-stage, due to a high recurrence rate
- Brachytherapy is typically combined with EBRT in an integrated treatment plan
- IMRT is preferred to minimize toxicities in definitive treatment of the pelvis
- Adaptive planning with IGRT, especially if IMRT is utilized, is encouraged
- Careful attention should be paid to dose tolerance of vaginal mucosa. The distal vagina has a lower tolerance than the proximal vagina
- Brachytherapy planning is highly individualized and should incorporate information from pre-EBRT and pre-brachytherapy imaging (preferably MRI), clinical drawings, fiducials, and exam findings
- Image-guided brachytherapy is strongly encouraged, with adaptation of volumes as tumor responds

Dose Frameworks:
- EBRT/IMRT to Pelvis: 45–50 Gy total, 1.8–2.0 Gy/fraction, 25–28 fractions, conventional fractionation, primary treatment component
- Gross Nodal Boost: 55–70 Gy EQD2, per fraction varies, fractions vary, integrated or sequential boost, grossly involved nodes
- Postoperative EBRT Boost: 54–60 Gy, per fraction varies, sequential boost, positive margins
- External Beam Boost (in place of brachytherapy): 65–70 Gy total, per fraction varies, sequential boost, poor brachytherapy candidates
- Brachytherapy HDR (after 45 Gy pelvis): 4.5–5.5 Gy x 5 fractions (or 7 Gy x 3, or 3 Gy x 9–10), typically after EBRT, invasive cancers
- Brachytherapy Intracavitary Alone (very early stage): 50–70 Gy EQD2 to vaginal surface, 5 Gy or 8 Gy per fraction, 8 fractions at 5 Gy or 5 fractions at 8 Gy, twice per week, very early stage

Approaches:
- EBRT/IMRT: 45–50 Gy in 1.8–2.0 Gy/fraction, concurrent cisplatin or carboplatin, for all stages, acute and late toxicities listed
- Brachytherapy (Intracavitary/Interstitial): 70–80 Gy EQD2 total to HR-CTV, after chemoradiation, all suitable patients, dose constraints to OARs
- Concurrent Chemoradiation (CCRT) with Brachytherapy: EBRT 45–50 Gy + concurrent cisplatin + brachytherapy boost

timeline.title: Regimen
timeline.body: EBRT/IMRT to Pelvis: 45–50 Gy in 1.8–2.0 Gy/fraction, 25–28 fractions. Followed by brachytherapy boost: 4.5–5.5 Gy x 5 fractions (or 7 Gy x 3, or 3 Gy x 9–10). Total treatment time ≤8 weeks.
